turns.content: Hey, I'm working on my side project and I have a struct with some fields. Should I derive Clone or Copy for it?

Good question! Deriving `Clone` or `Copy` is straightforward — just use `#[derive(Clone)]` or `#[derive(Copy)]`. But before you do, remember: deriving these traits only works if all fields implement those traits too. For example, if your struct has a `String`, which implements `Clone`, then deriving `Clone` will work fine.
Ah, I see. So derive automatically handles the Clone implementation for me?
Exactly! Deriving generates an implementation that calls `.clone()` on each field. But be careful — if any fields don't implement `Clone`, you'll get a compile error.
Okay, so if I have a generic struct with Copy types, deriving works fine?

Yes! Deriving on generics that implement `Copy` is safe. But wait — here's the tricky part: if your generic type parameter isn't itself `Copy`, you'll get an error even if you derive `Copy`. For example, deriving `Copy` on a struct with a non-Copy field will fail.
That makes sense. So what should I do if that happens?
If you need to implement `Copy` manually, just write the implementation yourself. The compiler won't generate one in this case. You must explicitly define how each field is copied.
